title: [Feature]: Evaluate a Google BigQuery sink
problem: BigQuery is a common analytics destination in connector ecosystems. nats-sinks does not yet provide a BigQuery sink for organizations using Google Cloud analytics stores.
proposal: Evaluate a BigQuery sink that writes normalized envelopes through an ingestion path with a clear success boundary, deterministic insert IDs or equivalent idempotency, bounded batches, and strict dataset and table validation.
users: Analytics teams and platform engineers that need JetStream event handoff into BigQuery while preserving nats-sinks delivery semantics.
delivery_semantics: The sink must never ACK messages. It may return success only after BigQuery has accepted the configured write operation and all row-level errors have been evaluated. Failed or partial inserts must raise framework errors unless explicitly handled through an idempotent duplicate policy.
security: The sink must validate project, dataset, and table identifiers; support workload identity where available; redact credentials; and avoid logging payloads or sensitive metadata by default.
acceptance: Research identifies the safest BigQuery ingestion method and idempotency strategy.
Functional requirements cover table mapping, insert IDs, duplicate behavior, metadata columns, schema handling, and payload envelopes.
Non-functional requirements cover batch sizing, quotas, retry classification, timeouts, and throughput tuning.
Security requirements cover identity, least privilege, redaction, and no payload logging.
Tests cover fake client success, row-level failure, duplicate handling, config validation, and no ACK on sink failure.
tests: Use fake clients for unit tests and gate live tests behind ignored local config. Include commit-then-ack contract tests.
documentation: If implemented, add a dedicated BigQuery sink documentation page and update core sink, configuration, security, operations, testing, roadmap, and changelog documentation.
closeout: Close after research decision or released connector. Include selected approach, tests, documentation, and known limitations.
